planting.note: Direct sow in cool weather, barely covering seeds with soil; Red Sails tolerates heat somewhat better than many lettuces and is slower to bolt, making it a good choice for extending the spring lettuce season. In zone 9, direct sow September–February for fall and winter production, or February–March for a brief spring window before summer heat.

directions: Sow seeds shallowly in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in cool weather; Red Sails is a 1985 All-America Selections winner red leaf lettuce that has remained one of the most widely grown and recommended loose-leaf lettuces for over 40 years, valued for its combination of attractive deep burgundy-red to bronze coloring, excellent flavor, and — most practically — its slow bolting characteristic that gives it several weeks longer in the garden before going to seed than most lettuces under warming spring conditions. The large, ruffled, wavy leaves are moderately to deeply red throughout, with a mild, sweet lettuce flavor that is pleasant and non-bitter even in moderate heat. As a loose-leaf type, Red Sails can be harvested by removing outer leaves continuously (cut-and-come-again), or the entire plant can be cut at once. Red Sails is excellent for mixed salad bowls and provides visual contrast when grown alongside green lettuce varieties. In zone 9, it excels in the fall-through-winter season (September–February) when temperatures are cool enough for crisp lettuce production.
sources: All-America Selections — Red Sails lettuce AAS winner 1985
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Lettuce Production Guide

common_name: Freckles Lettuce (Forellenschluss Romaine, Trout's Back Lettuce)

planting.note: Direct sow in cool weather with seeds barely covered; Freckles / Flashy Trout's Back is a loose-head romaine type that performs best in cool temperatures and is an excellent fall-through-spring crop in zone 9 (September–March).

directions: Sow seeds shallowly in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in cool weather; Freckles lettuce (Austrian name: 'Forellenschluss', meaning 'Trout's Back', also sold as 'Flashy Trout's Back') is a traditional Austrian/Central European heirloom romaine-type lettuce with one of the most distinctive and beautiful appearances in the lettuce world — pale green, slightly upright loose-romaine leaves dramatically spotted and splashed with burgundy-red markings in irregular patterns that genuinely resemble the markings of a brook trout's back. Despite its ornamental appearance, Freckles is an excellent eating lettuce with the substantial, crisp, slightly crunchy texture of romaine and mild, sweet, pleasant flavor without bitterness. The visual impact makes it outstanding for decorative vegetable gardens and for using fresh as a centerpiece lettuce. Freckles is moderately heat-tolerant for a lettuce and somewhat slow to bolt, extending the spring harvest window. An outstanding conversation-piece heirloom lettuce that combines genuine beauty with excellent eating quality.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Forellenschluss/Freckles lettuce variety notes

common_name: Red Pontiac Potato (Red Skin Waxy Potato)

planting.note: Plant certified seed pieces 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 plant January–February for May harvest. Red Pontiac is a late-season potato that performs well in the South's cool winter window, producing large, round tubers that are harvested before summer heat terminates the season.

directions: Plant certified seed pieces 2–3 inches deep, 4–6 weeks before last frost, after cutting to egg size with 1–2 eyes and allowing cut surfaces to cure for 1–2 days. Red Pontiac is the most widely grown red-skinned potato in the United States for over 50 years — a late-season, round, medium to large potato with smooth, dark red skin and white, waxy to slightly waxy flesh with moderate moisture content that holds its shape when boiled (making it excellent for potato salad, boiled potatoes, and roasting) while also mashing reasonably well. The bright red skin is attractive when left on for roasted preparations, and the firm texture keeps it together in soups and stews. Red Pontiac produces large, impressive tubers and is very productive in a wide range of soils, making it a reliable choice for home gardeners who want attractive, versatile red potatoes with a genuine all-purpose cooking character. In zone 9, plant in late January or early February and harvest in late April through May before summer heat causes plant decline.
sources: University of Maine Cooperative Extension — Potato Variety Guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Potato Production Guide

planting.note: Plant certified seed pieces 4–6 weeks before last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic, loose soil; in zone 9 plant January–February for April–May harvest before summer heat sets in. Mountain Rose is a mid-season variety well-suited to zone 9's cool winter potato-growing window.

directions: Plant certified seed pieces 2–3 inches deep 4–6 weeks before last frost, after curing cut surfaces for 1–2 days; Mountain Rose is a striking and delicious red-fleshed potato — the skin is red, but when cut, the flesh is also vivid rose-red to pink throughout, distinguishing it from standard red-skinned potatoes (like Red Pontiac) that have white flesh inside. The rosy-red flesh is high in anthocyanin antioxidants (the same pigments that color red cabbage, blueberries, and beets) and retains some color through cooking, making baked, mashed, and roasted Mountain Rose potatoes visually dramatic. The flavor is rich and earthy with good potato character; the texture is medium-moist and moderately waxy, excellent for baking, roasting, and boiling. Mountain Rose is a versatile all-purpose potato that performs well in both Southern cool-season production and in more northerly climates, and its beautiful color makes it outstanding for farmers' markets and gourmet home cooking. The red flesh fades somewhat during boiling or steaming but is better maintained when roasted or baked.
sources: Oregon State University Extension — Specialty Potato Varieties
University of Maine Cooperative Extension — Potato Variety Guide

common_name: Musquée de Provence Pumpkin (Fairytale Pumpkin, Cheese Pumpkin)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; Musquée de Provence is a large, sprawling Cucurbita moschata pumpkin requiring substantial space (15–20 feet vine length) and a long, warm growing season of 95–115 days, making it well-suited to the Gulf South's extended warm season.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in hills (3–4 seeds per hill, thinned to 2) after last frost in warm, rich soil; Musquée de Provence is a spectacular heirloom pumpkin from the Provence region of southern France that produces deeply ribbed, flat-lobed, heavily textured fruits that turn from dark green to a rich tan-bronze at maturity, resembling giant rounds of aged cheese (it is often sold under the marketing name 'Fairytale Pumpkin' in the US). The large fruits (10–20 pounds, sometimes to 30 pounds) are as stunning ornamentally as they are culinarily valuable — the deep orange, dense, sweet flesh is comparable to butternut squash in flavor (Musquée de Provence is Cucurbita moschata, the same species as butternut) with excellent sweetness, minimal stringiness, and superb flavor when roasted, pureed into soups, or made into pie. The thick skin and hard rind give outstanding storage life (6–12 months in a cool, dry location), and the impressive size and dramatic appearance make the fruits valuable for both Thanksgiving decorations and serious cooking. Well-adapted to the heat and humidity of Gulf South summer production and a rewarding long-season crop in zone 8–11.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cucurbit Production Guide
